chunks.text: Okay. Hi, Jo Jo. Hey, how are you? Very well, thank you. Good. I think we're just waiting for Mike. Can we start with two, no? Yeah. Yeah. With quorum's two. Okay. We might just keep going, Jo, because otherwise we're going to run out of time and the quorum's two. Thank you. Ka pai. Okay.

Just need to turn his camera on, so yeah. Hi, Nick. Welcome to the- Hello... welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee. Hey look, I'm just conscious that we've only got five minutes, so you can take your submission as read, but give us an introduction and then please, ideally leave a couple of minutes for questions. That'd be great.

Yeah, sure. No problem. Look forward to it So, thank you for reading my submission. As I said there, I do oppose the bill. My name is Nick Hoyle, and I would say I am somewhat fairly connected within the fishing space, fishing industry. I run a podcast. We've got about, oh, I don't know, 10,000 followers, something like that.

And I'm talking to a lot of fishermen a lot of the time, and there is genuine concern for the fishery right now. Whether it's up and down the country, I speak to people from all the way in the far, far north, right the way down to East Cape and beyond, and there is genuine concern, and concerns that the bill that has been put forward doesn't address the real issues with the fishery right now and only serves to feed more into the challenges that have been caused through the overfishing and the commercial fishing use of that fishery.

So, I feel like this resource is a public resource and it should be treated as such, not as just a money basket to feed foreigners. So that's kind of my statement on it. You've read my formal submission. Yeah. Excellent. Jo, do you have a question?

Yeah. Jo, just what do you mean by not just a money basket to feed foreigners? Can you elaborate on that, please? Well, so yeah, absolutely. So, if you look at the fish that is caught commercially, more than 90% of it is sent offshore. Why are we feeding people overseas when people that want to buy fish here in the supermarkets are paying crazy prices per kilo for fish that is sent offshore and is sold for cents on the dollar oftentimes just for the volumes that they're selling. I mean, the kahawai that's caught and sent to

Australia for crab bait. Come on. It's embarrassing. I've seen them out there. I've been out here in the gulf and you see these purse seine vessels catching all the bait that gets sold offshore to nations and it's like, man, no wonder our game fishery is suffering.

I talk to game fishermen, and the marlin just aren't coming because there's nothing for them to eat. So they're staying away, and that's if they even make it through the EEZ. But that's another whole conversation. The last three seasons on the east coast, the game fishing has been decimated. And you see these spotter planes come across, and then within an hour or so, there's these purse seiners taking the bait, and the fish are gone.

And there's more money in sport fishing and recreational boats than there is in sending it off for cents on the dollar overseas. It's crazy. Thank you. Thank you. So you talked about your-- congratulations on your podcast with 10,000 followers. So what are the general sentiments that you're getting back from them, much along the same lines as your submission?

I recently had a ex-fisheries observer on as a guest, and it just blows my mind the amount of waste, the amount of stuff. But people want this content. There's a real thirst for understanding fishing and the fishery and I would say I do put a lot of focus on recreational side of things, not the commercial sector.

But when I talk to people, they are seeing that the fishery's not in the state that it was and we're constantly told by the minister that, "Oh, we've got a world-class leading quota management system." Then why are we closing scallop beds, and why are we closing crayfish areas, and why are we having things, like I say, like a game fishery that's depleted if we're world-leading in our management? That's not world-leading. It's misleading.
Thank you very much. That's a really good point actually, Nick. Thank you. And thanks very much for taking the time to talk to us today. Yeah, appreciate it. Thank you. Cheers. Bye.

Okay, next up we have Craig Outen. Outen? Outen. It's Louisa George. Oh. Okay. Louisa George, is it? Kia ora. That's right, Louisa. Oh, got you. Hi, Louisa. I'm sorry. So we've got Jo here too, and we've just got five minutes, so it's not very long, but if you could make your remarks pretty brief so we've got time to ask a couple of questions, that would be awesome.

Fire away Sure. Thanks for the opportunity to speak today. I'm speaking as a recreational fisherwoman. I don't have a podcast, but that was really cool to hear Nick speak. I've got a family with a deep lifelong connection to the ocean here in Aotearoa. I'm based here in Raglan, Whangaroa. Fishing for us and getting out on the ocean is such a major part of how we spend time together, how I feed my family, connect with the environment. My 16-year-old son, he's a mad keen fisherman.

Him and his mates have got boats. They go out fishing, and to see that sense of pride when he provides for his family is just really awesome. I am concerned, which is why I normally wouldn't put my hand up to speak at the select committee. I would do a submission. But I am keen to get my voice out there to say, it's harder to catch fish in places where it used to be reliable.

No, we're not the only ones either. Some of the members of Parliament even referenced that they are finding it hard to catch a fish during the first reading of the Fishery Amendment bill, which I perked up my ears. And so from where I stand, the current system isn't really delivering that abundance that's associated with Aotearoa and the stories from our older generations.

So when we see that fish abundance declining in lots of places, it feels like the Fisheries Amendment bill is looking to weaken some of the protections or lower the standard of precaution in fisheries management. I also picked up on something that Shane Jones, Honorable Shane Jones said at the first reading as well about not going back to relitigate and look over past decisions, and I think good management should not be afraid to reflect, reassess, and adjust when outcomes aren't meeting expectations. Adaptive management is not a weakness.

It's responsible stewardship. So when I look at this bill, I'm worried that instead of strengthening the system and improving sustainability and improving things for the future, it actually is risking weakening what we've got further. So, while we've seen a signal back down around removing that minimum fish size protections, that those changes were proposed at all has deeply concerned a lot of the recreational fisher people, including me, around the direction of the legislation.

Fish are not an economic asset. They're a public resource that belongs to all New Zealanders, including our future generations. So we need to make sure that we've got strong public accountability and public trust. So reduced transparency around fisheries monitoring and decision making concerns me.

Changes that appear to place greater emphasis on economic outcomes over long-term sustainability, that concerns me. Shortened time frames for the public to challenge decisions and changes that could further disadvantage recreational fisher people like me, who already feel like we're not really seeing the-- we've got the short end of the stick a little bit.

So yeah, that's a quick summary. I'm not against change, but I am against change that moves us further away from healthy, abundant fisheries. And I'd like us to be aiming for more fish in the water, not fewer. More confidence in the system and the governance, not less. And for stronger protections.

Yeah. Oh, thank you very much, Louisa. We've reached time, but thank you, and great to hear the story about your son being a keen fisherman, too. Well done to him. Thank you. Appreciate the time. Awesome. Thank you. Ka kite anō. Ka kite.
I've got Rowan. I think Susan's busy at the minute, but- No, I'm here. Sorry... we've got five minutes to hear your submission. There she goes. All right.

Okay. Thank you. My name is Rowan Bullock. I live in Katikati, Western Bay of Plenty. Keen recreational fisherman. Have been since I was a young lad and also have had the opportunity to be in countries such as Romania and South Korea, where I have witnessed the effects of overfishing and exploitation, if you like, of their natural fisheries to the point where basically everything's banned.

But in terms of the amendments and the submission, thank you for letting us have this opportunity. It's a good reflection of our democracy. But in my view, these amendments actually seek to facilitate an unsustainable practice, which is bottom trawling. We're writing legislation around the fact that they can't discriminate the catch. We can't disseminate what we're catching, so we just make a catchall process, which I think is counter to good management of the fishery.

We have a syndrome in the Firth of Thames called milky flesh in snapper, and the department says it's because of starvation. Now, you don't have to take a very deep think to think, "Well, why is it the snapper's starving in a so-called Coastline of Plenty?" Well, it's because we're ripping the environment out of the sea floor with bottom trawling.

But as opposed to Mycoplasma bovis, which we spent over a billion dollars eradicating, nobody seems to spend any money trying to find what this problem is in snapper, because I don't think they want to know the answer. So I think, again, the proposals seek to reduce the accountability, not to enhance it.Now we grow kiwifruit here, and we're subject to global gap requirements, which basically looks at all of the facets of our business, from fruit quality to management to governance, and we're accountable. If we don't pass

those thresholds, Zespri won't take our fruit, and we're happy with that. And I'm sure other primary producers are also subject to similar scrutiny through places such as Fonterra. So I guess the point is, we want to see our fishery managed for generations well past ours, not just what's going to turn a dollar in the short term.

Bottom trawling, really, we need to take a look at ourselves as a country. Nobody else apparently does it. And the act does say that in 13F, that the minister should be taking into account the environment when approving these systems for fishing. And we are destroying the environment, not enhancing it.

So, we can change from fishing types. I don't oppose commercial fishing at all, but target species through things like longlining rather than just netting. And I think we really need to get serious about managing it. As earlier speakers have said, there's a lot of money in recreational fishing, tourism, and whatever that we seem to be happy to destroy. It's just very sad.

Thank you very much, Ron. Jo, do you have a question? No, but thank you for your submission, Ron. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you very much. That was awesome. Thank you. Thanks for taking time to talk to us today. Cheers. Bye. Bye. Mark Modra.
He'll be marking you in now as well. Okay, thanks.

Hi, Mark. Yes. I'm Sue Redmayne. You're here with Jo Luxton as well. We realize we've only got a short amount of time, so if you want to just make a statement, and then leave some time for some questions, that'd be fantastic. Thank you very much. Yes, good afternoon, and thanks for taking the time to consider my submission.

I am a long time listener, but first time caller. Probably prompted by the passion that I can see others have for this matter. Look, I'm a scientist, postgraduate qualified with over 20 years' experience practicing in science, and while not in fisheries, I understand science enough to sort of see the potential for manipulation of scientific data to meet industry objectives.

I'm strongly opposed to the bill as it stands. And that view's shared by many of the people I talk to, friends, family, colleagues. I've been a keen recreational fisher for longer than I can remember. And yeah, I just want to present to you a few empirical examples of the importance of recreational fishing.

The fishery, the importance of it to others outside of the industry. My 85-year-old father loves to retell the same stories, and one of those is when he first borrowed some scuba gear when it was a new thing. I'm not sure how long ago that was, but safe to say many, many years. And he went underwater and he describes the environment that he saw as being like Kelly Tarlton's.

Safe to assume that that means, due to the abundance and types of fish, rather than them being in an enclosure or cage. And look outside of probably Goat Island. I've never experienced that. And then, my two young children this summer, took them out. They caught their first fish and it was so exciting for them, and it was super exciting for me to see that. And we need that opportunity to be available for future generations, without an end date for that to sort of happen.

It's been stated by the few people that I've seen speaking just before me that the current system, the fact that we're closing fisheries off demonstrates its failings, right? It's not working. And fisheries are closed pretty late and often probably won't recover, certainly not to the state that they were before.

And it certainly appears that the proposed changes are going to make it even worse. They're not changes being put in place to increase environmental safeguards. They're eroding the safeguards that are already there. Yet that empirical evidence suggests that we need to go the other way, so that we can have a sustainable fishery and sustainable meaning, for the long term, not sustaining the pockets of a few for the short term.

Oh, thank you. I've got a question, if that's okay. You mentioned the manipulation of scientific data to meet industry objectives, and I know one of my colleagues on the committee, Rachel Boyack, this morning talked about her concern about the lack of available data. I just wondered if you could expand on that, on your concern about the manipulation of the data.

Look, so yes, there is a lack of data and in some cases, decisions are being made that should be much more cautious because of that lack of data rather than the other way around. But where we have data, often it's actually collected by industryIt's not all independently collected information. So that's why I make that comment, and I'm not saying that it has been manipulated.

I'm merely pointing out that I understand how it can be, and therefore the dangers of relying on scientific information that is not objective. Thank you very much. Do you have a question, Jo? Okay. Thank you very much for your time, Mark. We really appreciate it.

And you did well for- All the best... yeah, and great being a first-time submitter. You're not alone, I can assure you. I bet. Thank you. Okay. Tim. Hi, Tim. I'm Suz. You've also got Jo Luxton here. You're spot on, good afternoon. You've got five minutes, which goes pretty quickly, so if you want to leave some time for a couple of questions- Ha ha... that'd be amazing.

100%, and look, yeah, I'm going to leave plenty of time for questions because I'm guessing you have read my submission. And look, there's probably, I guess, only three sort of key, I guess, points that I just want to sort of, I guess, sort of stress and I call them sort of more the macro level points that I want to make. And that is simply that this bill as it stands, the only economic benefit is to commercial fishing, but it's not actually long-term sustainable in that regard alone.

And I really want to, I guess, sort of zoom in onto the economic benefits of recreational fishing and what then translates into a significant spend in local economies. And look, if we're trying to get social license from our communities for a sustainable fishery long term, we actually have to have, I guess, an overarching economic structure and construct which actually allows great community involvement, which is obviously happens through recreational fishing rather than commercial. And it's not about cutting commercial out, but you have to

have a fisheries environment that allows both to thrive. And look, I guess, for someone that's had the privilege of living in paradise in Bream Bay in Northland for all of my life, I can just testify to when I was a kid and up into my early 20s, Bream Bay, we had big, big workups of kahawai, kingfish, birds flocking everywhere, year after year after year. We don't see those anymore, and that is a direct result of intensive commercial fishing and particularly purse seining, which is just simply scooping the bay clean.

So my plea really is to say, look, do please consider the long-term economic benefit of a thriving recreational fishery, which can only happen if commercial is allocated its fair share and nothing more. That's the, I guess, the bulk of my submission. Very happy to take some questions and expand on any of that for you.

Jo, you got anything? Yeah, I do have a question. I just wanted to ask you about your line that says you reject any attempt to limit public access to camera footage through the Official Information Act. And we've had a few conversations about this, and one of the things that was raised was that when you have other events, say for example, a crime that's on the street and CCTV footage captures it or in a store where someone, maybe a staff member, has taken something illegally, that footage is not available to the public. And I wondered why

you would like the public to have continued access to this type of footage, because does it not then perhaps put workers in a compromised situation when they might be seen publicly and people can identify them in that way? Do you not think that there's some sort of privacy for workers needed here?

Just a- Yep. Look, great question. And I guess there's two parts to that answer or two part answer to that. The first part is, look, in releasing the footage, because obviously it's not real-time footage, so the technology is absolutely there to number one, screen and mask individuals. So the footage is more about, I guess, the access to what are the practices that are going on.

And so I think that's the first part is that you can absolutely bake privacy sort of requirements and, to your point, staff safety into that. But I think on the other end of the scale, when I look at what floats around on social media every single day of people's recording of, should we say, unsavory events in local streets and pubs and everything else like that.

There's, I guess, a bigger question mark anyway around what constitutes privacy in this day and age through the private filming and releasing via social media of things that are happening on a day-to-day basis. Thank you. Thank you. You talked about this trying to get a social license from our communities, which resonated with me and I just wondered, what do you think needs to be done in terms of commercial fisheries to get that social license?

Sure. Well, I think the first thing is that obviously the parts that I'm opposed to in the bill is what is essentially almost like a wholesale license to the commercial sector to take what they like, and not be punished for reckless and unnecessary wastage of resource through small size catch and bycatch. And the second part of it is that there needs to be a mechanism in place which recognizes and quantifies the economic benefit of recreational fishing alongside commercial.

We've got plenty of data of what the economic benefit of commercial fishing is, but we have zero data of what the actual economic benefit of recreational fishing truly is. Oh, thank you. Well, Matt Watson put a number on it this morning, I think, in his submission. He said it was worth about 1.6 billion to the GDP. Yeah. But you're right.

I'll happily ask my good mate Brad Olsen that one. I'm sure he'll find a number. Thank you very much, Tim. We appreciate your time. Wonderful. Thanks so much for having me. Cheers. Bye. Right. Toshi Rapana.
Is the mic on? He is. So it's video on.

Oh. Turned it off. Hello, can you hear me? Yes. Kia ora, Toshi. Kia ora. We can't see you, but we can hear you. Oh. Oh, okay. Oh, I'm not sure why that's happening, but it's okay. Okay, that's fine. Okay. We've just got five minutes, so if you could keep your introductory remarks relatively brief, if you want to leave some time for questions, that would be fantastic.

Yeah, sure. The floor is yours. So kia ora, my name is Toshi Rapana. I'm a recreational fisher and free diver from Whakatane. Mm-hmm. And I'm speaking today in opposition to aspects of the Fisheries Amendment Bill. I strongly support monitoring, accountability, and enforcement within our fisheries system. Better data collection and monitoring tools can help improve transparency and ensure rules are being followed properly.

However, I do not support any proposal that weakens protections for our fisheries, in particular around undersized catch and sustainability. As someone who spends time in the water gathering kaimoana for my family, I have seen the decline in accessible seafood stocks.

Since returning to Whakatane after living in Wellington for many years, I have not found a single legal sized pāua in the areas I can safely and legally access, including areas along the coast such as Te Kaha and Omaio. This concerns me greatly because it reflects the wider pressure our marine ecosystems are under. I am also deeply concerned about the spread of kina barrens throughout Whakatane and down the east coast. In my view, this is closely linked to depleted crayfish populations. Crayfish play an important role in controlling our kina numbers, and

when crayfish decline, the kinas overgraze the reefs and they destroy the vegetation, which many species rely on. This bill should be focused not only on managing extraction, but also on restoring and protecting marine ecosystems in the future. I do not support allowing commercial operators to retain undersized fish.

Fish need the opportunity to grow and reproduce to maintain healthy stocks. Recreational fishers are required to follow strict size limits, and commercial operators should be held to at least the same standard. I would also like to see stronger accountability measures included in the fisheries management.

If commercial operators catch undersized fish or exceed their quota, there should be meaningful consequences. I believe an undersized catch should still count towards their quota, even if it's returned alongside penalties for quota breaches. This would encourage better fishing practices and reduce unnecessary environmental harm. I also have concerns about destructive fishing practices such as bottom trawling, which damages not only fish stocks, but our entire marine ecosystems and seabed habitats. More sustainable methods need to be

prioritized moving forward. Finally, I would like to see greater transparency from both the government and commercial operators about how fisheries are being managed and what is being done to restore marine ecosystems. I also support reinvesting a portion of commercial fishing profits into restoration initiatives such as kina removal and crayfish recovery.

Our moana is our taonga, and the decisions made through this bill will impact what is left for our tamariki and our mokopuna in the future. Thank you. Thank you very much, Toshi. That was very comprehensive. Jo, do you have a question? Okay, Jo's on mute, but that's okay. Thank you very much for your time.

I was just quickly going to talk to the-- I understand that the supporters of the bill would argue that the idea behind making commercial fishers keep the undersized fish is that 99% of them die anyway, as opposed to recreational fishers that are fishing with a hook and line and from shallow water, where the survivability is much better of those that are returned to the sea. And that they do actually have to count them against their quota.

So they would argue that it might make them change their behavior if it's going to reduce their profit margin. If, say, 20% of their catch is undersized and they're having to just rely on the money they make from 80%. So how would you argue that? To be honest, I feel like commercial operators need to come up with their own solutions because I feel as though they've been given too much leeway, in terms of their catch and their quotas.

And in terms of how this might affect their profits, I guess that's up to them to determine and to decide on. Okay. Thank you very much for your time, Toshi. Much appreciated. Thank you. Okay. Derek Paul

Kia ora. Kia ora, Derek. Tēnā koutou katoa. Ko Derek Paul toku ingoa. I wish to submit on the Fisheries Amendment Bill. Thank you for this opportunity. I've been a recreational fisher since I was three years old, and I've been involved in fisheries management since 2005. Sat as a board member and a VP on the Recreational Fishing Council and the New Zealand Angling and Casting Association.

I've been a warranted fisheries officer with MPI, and I've sat on recreational forums for MPI, and I sat on ministerial advisories representing the public to healthy and abundant fisheries. In 2011, I founded a fishing magazine that ran for seven years before we sold it. It was provided to the public for free to help engage and enable better fishing experiences for the people.

I wrote my first fishery submission in 2007 on the then proposed Marine Protected Areas legislation, which is still not closed. There've been many more, and there have been many that have been completely ignored by those who make decisions. I've submitted to ministers from Jim Anderton, Phil Heatley, David Clark, Nathan Guy, Stuart Nash, Rachel Brooking, and now Shane Jones.

Everything I do in the realm of fisheries has been to elevate the recreational fisher's experience and to push the ministry to make changes that increases the abundance of all fish species. I've been around fish for some time, and quite frankly, I've probably had more experience in this space than any of the MPs sitting on the panel today. So when I submit this bill, I'm not a bot, I'm not just a noisy voice, I'm not a Yamaha, Kawasaki, blah blah, who's catastrophizing, as the minister likes to label us, but I'm pointing out the significant concerns of Kiwis who fish

for food and fun. I strongly oppose the Fisheries Amendment Bill. Specifically, I oppose the changes that reduce the public's access to camera footage collected on commercial vessels for the purpose of compliance in any form, and the heavy-handed penalties that the bill proposes should this be breached. The minister's defense that the fisheries industry operates in the most privacy invasive environment is in itself catastrophizing. There's little, if any, identifying information on these cameras. The workers are not being filmed in their galley or bedrooms, et

cetera. I think the minister might like to reconsider his hyperbolic statements that obscure the truth. The taking of small fish is wrong, and we should be doing everything we can to minimize this. While I personally believe that all fish should be landed regardless of size, with strictly enforced deemed values or similar on fish landed below a specific size, under no circumstances should fish be returned to the sea unless specifically allowed for, such as we have for sharks, billfish, and strong, live kingfish.

Let's not beat around the bush. It's wasteful and a tax on the marine environment. The bill muddies the water around this issue and allows fishers to dump or keep small fish. Simply, all fish must be landed, and it's up to the minister to set disincentives for small fish being landed, not just keep giving free rein to fishers or their quota holders that tell them to, to decide when to keep or dump. The bill includes provisions to carry over quota and lock TACC and other anti-abundance issues. These appear to only serve the commercial industry and do

not meet the requirements of the Fisheries Act to maintain sustainable fisheries. There have been submissions by others, and there will be by others, who've delved into the science around these activities, and I cede to their greater knowledge. The practice of setting long, that's over one year, TACCAs and carrying over quota breaches the precautionary principle and unnecessarily risks the health of fish stocks.

There's nothing in this legislation that will specifically drive improved practice. Nothing that meets the minister's obligation under the Fisheries Act, where the opening line states that the purpose of the act is to provide for the utilization of fisheries while ensuring sustainability. It is clear that this bill will further undermine sustainability. Aotearoa has the opportunity to actually be what the commercial sector repeatedly tells us we are, a world leading fishery. This bill will not deliver that.

I've never seen a piece of legislation in the fishery space that is this anti-public and likely to cause meaningful long-term harm to the environment. My request is that the bill be withdrawn, and before a replacement is drafted in any way, that the minister instructs his ministry to consult with the public meaningfully. If you want to improve returns for our country, the answer is not to allow practices that have demonstrably negative impacts on our stocks.

The answer is a modern, abundant, and ecosystem-focused practice that sees the public, tangata whenua, and commercial fishers benefit from more fish in the water, lower cost to access, and a better international reputation that drives real value, not just $1.90 a kilo export of whole frozen kahawai to Namibia and 5 cent a kilo mackerel to Wallis and Futuna.
Thank you so much, Derek. We've actually reached time, but thank you very much for taking the time to talk to us today. Thanks.

Dale Breakaway. This is really annoying. Hello. Kia ora, Dale. Hi. You've got five minutes, which seems to go pretty quickly, and you can take your submission as read, but if you want to leave some time for questions, that'd be great. Fantastic.

The floor is yours. I'm happy to give a minute of mine to Derek because that was a fantastic submission, and I would like to strongly echo what he has said as my first point. But thank you for the time to speak. I'm a very proud New Zealander. I'm a spear fisherman and a free diver, and I've spent a significant amount of time in the water in New Zealand and overseas.

AndMy reason for wanting to talk on this issue is because I've seen these ecosystems being destroyed in real time. As Toshi noted a couple of talks ago, the spread of kina barrens is clear if you're one of these people who's in the water regularly, and I've noted that in Abel Tasman over my couple of decades diving there, where there used to be a thriving ecosystem and now it is effectively a desert.

We know why this happens. It's a symptom of overfishing, as she quickly stated, and it's just one example of what is truly happening in our marine environment and the impact we're having. It's effectively a broken ecosystem, and it's not for the betterment of ourselves as recreational fishermen, nor for the commercial industry, nor for anyone in New Zealand.

So we know what happens from overfishing. History's shown us that the commercial industry needs strong controls on traffi, et cetera, and with a good ecosystem, with a healthy ecosystem, comes the maximum sustainable catch and the maximum value for New Zealand as a whole. So these things are very well aligned.

We have a healthy ecosystem, we have a good fishery. So I have no issue with commercial fishing. As Derek mentioned, people are quick to label opponents of this bill as anti commercial fishing, and that's simply not true. But there's better ways to do this, and like I said, a healthy ecosystem actually gets us the best outcome.

The omission of anything about bottom trawling in this bill is a massive red flag, and if we really wanted to do what's best for the environment, and like I say, what's best for actually making a good, strong, valuable fisheries industry, then we would be addressing that. We cannot have a healthy fishery if we are bulldozing the sea floor.

It's incredibly destructive, and yet this bill ignores it entirely. I'm also deeply concerned by the paid for nature of this piece of legislation. The relationship between New Zealand First, the minister, and various industry parties here is incredibly clear.

Spoken about publicly at length, and it's clear that this piece of legislation is not for New Zealand, it is for industry. It's effectively bought and paid for, and that is simply not how our government should function. It's not democratic, and I'm frankly deeply concerned about that aspect.

The $50,000 fine, which we know is a gag, really, and the other anti-democratic aspects of removing footage from OIA requests and keeping the minister from being able to be scrutinized by the public with this very short two-week period is just further evidence on that. So sorry, I know we're at time. I'll wrap up.

There's various other parts of the government, as you well know, have sounded their own concerns about this. The whole thing is a bad faith exercise. We do need to make fisheries change. There are aspects here that should be considered and taken into another bill, but this bill must be strongly rejected.

The anti-democratic aspects must be strongly rejected, and we should come into the future with something that addresses these massive issues, the decline we've already seen, keeps this resource for all New Zealanders, and keeps us in the process and keeps everyone, including the industry, accountable.

Thank you very much. Thank you so much, Daryl. Just very quickly before you go, you talked about there's some aspects of the bill that you'd like to see introduced in another piece of legislation. What were those? Which were the bits that you liked? Well, you asked two questions ago about this removal of size limits, and as you noted, many of these fish, smaller fish caught are dead.

Obviously, we need to address the fishing methods, if it's bottom trawling, things like that, we need to do things that are more selective. But where we are catching things that are outside of the intended size range, then yes, throwing them back is a zero value add for almost anyone. So, and there's certainly cases where catching small fish is actually the best way to remove fish from an ecosystem, right?

This is a big problem with recreational fishermen who love smashing the that they can find. That fish is super valuable as a breeding individual. So there's somewhere in there where the optimum is, and there's definitely some rebalancing we can do there. But again, it has to come out of the quota, and there has to be strong protections for a breeding population, and that's what isn't currently in there.

I was brought up around fishing, bay of plenty. Grandparents and father and friends out on the boat, off the beach. I dive. I take people out. I love eating fish. I love buying fish. But in the current form, I'm really worried about this bill, and I can see that we can do a lot better. It kind of casts me back to the early '80s where we had peer trawling going along the coasts very close to shore, and we know the damage it was doing to inshore fishing at that time.

I feel like we're kind of going back to those unknown days and allowing fishing happening in the remote areas, and not seeing what's going on with that. I lived in Hong Kong for 15 years and traveled in Asia a lot, and minimum catch size for both fish length, fish species, number of those species, are all really important.

When you come back here and see the benefits of having some of those limitations on means that you don't get the situation where people are catching what's basically a baby fish that should nowhere near reproductive age, and it should be a case of that should be growing up to at least reproductive age. And not having fish being caught to feed other fish as the primary target for what happens with quotas that I've seen in those areas.

And besides the commercial or recreational fishing, we have to make sure we have that healthy ecosystem. As we know, there's so many feedback loops, whether it's sea birds, marine mammals, fish stocks, plants in the oceans. We've heard about kin bearings, all of that. So when we look at quotas and the processor who's in there, it's not about a resource that... And I use the word resource.

Resource is the word that we think of as something we can just take, and we shouldn't be thinking of that as humans. We've got to think of the other planet mates that we've got here, whether that's the bird, the fish, the other fish species that are not target. We need to take them into account when we're doing our quota and how we're fishing. This bill, to me, appears to reward the laggards and not the forward-thinking fishing folks that are doing really good, really cool, sustainable, purposefully caught.

They go out with a catch list. They know where that fish is going, catching and buying it, rather than the bulk fishing. And we often fall into this in our primary industries in New Zealand of going for the bulk rather than the high quality known source that we know food security can give us such a better return. UmMonitoring the fishering activities, the camera one and the limits on that, we need to have those cameras or technology in place that not only looks above water, but what's going on as it's coming up and on the bottom destruction.

I think we can use our technology better, and we shouldn't be limiting access to that info, but we should be expanding the technology available to basically what the fishing guys are using to catch these things, what else is in there, and getting that data spread back to help more understanding and better balancing of people's understanding of what can be taken and what can't.

The factory processing at sea, that is just like, it should have the same demands as if it was processed on the land in New Zealand. And those quotas should not allow to roll over to a next year. The minimum quota, if you don't get it, that's it. The same as recreational fishers. Imagine if we changed that, so every day you could roll on to what you hadn't caught the other day for a whole year.

That's a really good point. So, I reject the bill in its current form. What do you want to do now? Oh, just come and take a seat in the middle there, and help yourself to a glass of water. And I think you've heard it all before, but you got five minutes, which goes really quickly. So the floor is yours. Right. So, my name's Bryce Helms. I'm here today as a lifelong angler, recreationally.

And yeah, want to express my complete opposition to this bill in its current format. Um, this bill prioritizes the profits of a few quota holders over the health of our oceans and the interest of over a million recreational fishermen in this country, and women. While I disagree with it in its entirety, in the interest of time, I'll try and limit it to a few specific sections.

So firstly, the minimum size limits in Section 67C and 67D. We've all seen and heard the response to this. It's the easiest to grasp as it's the most egregious example of putting commercial interests above those of recreational. Minimum size limits are set to allow fish to reach maturity and breed before being harvested.

Harvesting immature fish before they can breed is biological sabotage and morally bankrupt when recreational fishermen have to abide by the same size limits. While this is a headline-grabbing clause, if it were a removal of limit, limit, minimum size limits combined with the requirement to land all catch with that counted against the TAC, I would support this.

However, combined with the rest of the bill, it's not that. It simply isn't. Which brings me to the second part. quota top-ups in Clause 14, Section 21. This clause mandates that the minister can adjust the total allowable catch upwards to account for fishing-related mortality revealed by cameras.

Camera trials showed huge increases in discards and dumping. TACs have been set using discard data that is clearly far below the actual figure, and the camera rollout will, by the Ministry's own admission, result in further increase in discards being recorded. Instead of reducing the TAC to account for this mortality or trying to reduce waste, this clause does the exact opposite.

Even worse is the ability to incrementally increase this allowance as the camera rollout progresses. And thirdly, legalizing the dumping of dead usable fish in Clause 32, Section 72A. This section removes the requirement that fish returned to the sea have a high likelihood of survival.

With the allowances in the previous clause meaning that these discards don't necessarily count against their quota. As long as they're recorded by a camera or reported, it's green-lit. They can effectively choose whether they keep a fish or not, undersized, oversized, whatever. They can discard those fish without that being allocated against their TAC because of these quota top-ups.

Fourthly, the shielding of onboard cameras from public oversight. The initial rollouts of these cameras have revealed huge underreporting of bycatch and discards. Specifically, MPI's data showed in the initial rollout, a 950% increase in kingfish discards, a 680% increase in dolphin catches, a 210% increase in total species discarded, and a 1,250% increase in snapper discards.

That's not accidental underreporting. That is systematic, that is deliberate, and that is skewing the data that these catch limits have been set using. Despite this, it's been revealed today that the minister went directly against official recommendations with these clauses. Time and time again, this industry has proven that it can't be trusted to self-regulate, and public oversight is necessary because the Ministry has failed to act on this underreporting. No one's been prosecuted because of it.

It's just been allowed and, in fact, rewarded with additional catch allowances to allow for this increase in discards. And lastly, the five-year TAC allowances. So I strongly oppose this, lacking annual reviews. And furthermore, it's inserted socio-economical factors as part of the reasoning for setting TACs rather than just biological and ecological and biomass standpoints.

Five-year limits would be disastrous. The collapse of the scallop fishery is the perfect example of this. Upon discovering two huge mother beds in the Hauraki Gulf, the Ministry's decision was not to protect these areas, but to instead increase the commercial quota by over 1,000%. Two years later, those beds are decimated. The fishery's closed. They're gone.

A five-year limit would have been absolutely disastrous in this case, even worse than the debacle that already unfolded. So just to conclude, I don't have the financial backing to be able to wine and dine the fisheries minister to make huge donations to his political party, but I do have an endless amount of passion for the health of our oceans. And it deeply saddens me to see our fisheries minister showing an open disdain for his constituents and resorting to bravado, bluster, and personal insults when challenged by those like myself with genuine concerns for our fisheries.

I guess that's understandable when he's never actually won a seat in Parliament. He's never been voted anywhere, but he acts like he's the mandate of the entire country. This bill is an attack on the Kiwi way of life and is only good for the profits of a select few quota holders. I urge the committee to reject this Fisheries Amendment Bill in its entirety and go back to the drawing board because this thing is an insane piece of legislation that does nothing for the health of our fisheries.

Thank you so much. Thank you for your considered submission, too. And thank you very-- We've reached time, but we really appreciate what you had to contribute, Bryce. Thank you very much. No worries. Yeah, great job. Thanks.
So do we have Harrison Rush or Jill Jackson?

Harrison. Hello. Am I coming over- You're all right... nice and clear? Yes, perfect. So you've got five minutes, which goes very quickly. So, and consider your submission read, so feel free to leave us some time at the end for questions if you'd like to do that. Thank you very much. You're welcome. Good afternoon, and thank you for the opportunity to speak.

My name is Harrison Rush, and I am a person who cares about our environment, our communities, and the future of Aotearoa, New Zealand. I do not support the Fisheries Amendment Bill. Growing up in New Zealand, the ocean has always been a part of my life. Some of my strongest memories are of the simple things: going fishing with my family, walking along the beach, and exploring rock pools.

These experiences are not just personal, they are a part of who we are as a nation. They connect us to our environment and to each other. This is why I believe we cannot risk damaging or endangering our oceans for the sake of larger commercial profits or increased tax revenue. We have a responsibility to ensure future generations can have that same relationship with our marine environment that I have had. They deserve to experience abundant sea life, not depleted ecosystems.

I strongly oppose this bill because it risks privatizing New Zealand's fish, a shared public resourceI am deeply concerned that these changes will result in fewer fish available for everyday New Zealanders to catch and feed their families. This bill weakens sustainability measures and prioritizes short-term profit for a small number of owners over the long-term health of our fisheries and the public interests.

I am also concerned about the erosion of transparency. Limiting public access to onboard camera footage under the Official Information Act undermines trust and accountability in commercial fishing practices. There are several specific provisions I oppose.

I oppose removing the minimum size limits for commercial catches. Fish must be allowed to breed before they are harvested. I do not support allowing more fish to be discarded overboard when cameras or observers are present. It sends the wrong message and encourages waste. I'm concerned about extending catch decisions to last up to five years with limited input.

That reduces oversight at the time when we need more. I oppose lowering the standard of information required to increase catch limits. We should be strengthening protections, not weakening them. I also oppose removing environmental safeguards that protect biodiversity and fish abundance.

We need more fish in the water, not fewer. Reducing penalties for over-catching, particularly for large deep water trawlers, removes an important deterrent. Without strong enforcement, rules lose their meaning. Ultimately, I strongly oppose any changes that move control of our fisheries further into private hands without regard for the wider public good. In closing, I wish to make the following recommendations. I urge that the Select Committee reject the Fisheries Amendment bill in full.

I recommend that the fisheries management prioritizes making fish available for local communities rather than favoring low-value exports. We should set higher targets for stock levels so populations are abundant, resilient, and sustainable for future generations. Finally, I recommend an inquiry into transitioning away from more destructive and indiscriminatory fishing methods towards more selective and sustainable practices. This is an opportunity to protect something that belongs to all of us, not just today, but for future
generations to come. Thank you very much for your time. Thank you so much, Harrison. We've actually reached time, but thank you very much for being part of the process. It's awesome.
Thank you. Jill Jackson.

Are Are we live still? Just needs to turn her camera on. Unmute. Kia ora tatou. Can you hear me? We can hear you, Jill, but could you please, if you've got a camera or you want to turn your camera on, if you can turn it on, that'd be great.

That's it. Perfect. I've done my best. Ka pai. Thank you. Kia ora tatou. Thank you for your attention and the opportunity to speak on my submission, which was short and sweet, because you're never quite sure if it's going to get through. I'm Jill Jackson. I'm a retired teacher and a gardener, and I live with my partner, Steve, who is beside me here.

We're not fishery experts by any means, but we do have some life experiences to draw on to flesh out our submission. Steve is an offshore master, and he was a commercial fisherman in Napier. And Steve saw a depletion of fishing stock from the 1960s onward that he describes as exponential, and he's seen the attitude of, "If I don't take it, someone else will," in the fishing industry.

Steve says independent public scrutiny of the cameras on boats is essential. I had a Royal Society Primary Teachers' Fellowship, which is a year's science sabbatical. And I worked, at the time, in Bream Bay Aquaculture Park, which is niwa, and observed the development of the aquaculture industry, raising pāua, hāpuku, and kingfish, which enlightened me greatly as to aquaculture.

Steve and I went fishing on Saturday, and we caught a healthy snapper and a couple of kahawai. In our subjective opinion, the Kaipara Harbour is in better condition than when we came here 15 years ago, and I think it's probably due to the massive efforts of Kaipara Moana Remediation, which is an organization which gets farmers on board and funds and encourages riparian and wetlands planting to prevent more silting up of the harbor, which has been quite bad over the years, by agricultural runoff.

So careful monitoring of the species that we catch can help us judge the health of the ocean, which provides us with so much more than food. The healthy ocean provides us with oxygen, acts as a carbon sink, and is a buffer against rising temperatures. The Kaipara Harbour has quite a number of us well-placed to oversee what's happening in it, but not so the open seas where our fisheries are busy.

They can't be observed by the general public, and we rely on robust legislation, quotas that are fair and allow for fish populations to regenerate, and cameras or observers to ensure no one's taking unfair advantage. And if fisheries do, they're taking from everyone. They're endangering fish stock, and present and future populations will sufferWe must be able to trust the enforcement of the legislation. The level of trust in the intentions of the Minister of Fisheries is virtually nil.

He hasn't encouraged trust in the care of the environment. There's ample evidence of depleted breeding stocks, disruption to seamounts by trawling, and we know it takes years to rebuild them. On its website, the Ministry for Environment lists the numbers of endangered marine species and notes that there's not enough known about many of them to be able to say whether they're endangered or not.

And without this kind of information, we're playing Russian roulette with the health of New Zealand's own unique marine biology. And add to that, the huge pressure put on our natural environments by other human activities, burning fossil fuels and the raising of the temperatures. It seems common sense to just say stop and protect and help regenerate what we have left. We won't have anything if we keep plundering it.

We're as much a part of the picture of a healthy ocean as any shark or mussel or mangrove or invertebrate. Human brains are thought to have developed in size because of a seafood diet during our evolution. I suggest we use those brains to understand we can't take more than the system can provide. I don't believe we can afford to relax any of the existing regulations.

I recommend the panel reject the Fisheries Amendment Bill in its entirety. Thank you very much, Jill and Steve. Look, we've reached time, but as a farmer, I'm grateful for you thanking the farmers for their riparian planting and the contribution that made to the health of the Kaipara Harbour, so good on you.

Thank you. Thanks for your time. Thank you. Oh, thank you very much. All right. Yeah. Back up to Auckland now. Oh, well, good on you for coming down. It's a real mission, but thank you. That's awesome. Worth the effort for this, so. Pardon? Worth the effort for this. Yeah, no, it's awesome. Thank you.
Cheers. See you.
Right.

Oh, Linda. Kia ora. Kia ora. Oh, kia ora. We can hear you. Thank you for this opportunity. I'm another person who opposes the bill in its entirety. Despite the quota management system, our fisheries are depleted, penguins are dying of starvation, and it's harder for families to catch a feed.

Fisheries New Zealand has recorded a decline in catch over the last two decades, with total catch well below the TAC. Instead of providing for more liberal catch rates and deemed values, the government should be examining the reasons for the significant decline and producing credible measures to reverse it.

But it's obvious that the TAC has been too high for too long, and assessment of most fish stocks has not been reviewed for decades. This lack of up-to-date data suggests there is no or inadequate management in the quota management system. Fish is expensive to buy. There is an insatiable world demand for it that cannot be reasonably met. I've lived in communities with significant deprivation that are mostly Māori and mostly poor.

The current fuel crisis is exacerbating the well-being of such people. Some have an old tinny or they go surf casting. This is the only way whānau can acquire fish. But there are stories we hear that it is more difficult to catch a feed for the family. Trawlers have been witnessed coming close inshore in the Eastern Bay of Plenty.

A while ago, I went to a public meeting at a local marae about Ngāti Awa's application for a mataitai around Motuhora. The area involved was pathetically small, but it was vigorously opposed by commercial fishers. We haven't heard anything more about it. This is the power of the commercial fishing industry, and I suggest to you that the proposals in this bill have been driven by commercial interests and any benefits arising will accrue to those interests only at the expense of non-commercial fishers and the environment.

Other examples show the difficulty of achieving better fisheries management and protection of the marine environment. The Tarakihi case, which I'm sure you're familiar with. The Motiti Maunga Rohe Trust here in the Bay of Plenty went to court to have some islands free from fishing. They succeeded, but it took 10 years and RMA amendments have been introduced to make this less likely in the future.

As a result of these examples and others, this minister is trying to stop the public from being a watchdog through removing opportunities for consultation and reducing time frames for lodging judicial review proceedings. The proposed ban on camera records being released under the OIA is an outright attack on that foundational act, which already protects the privacy of individuals, and the proposed fines are outrageous.

As most of the footage is not reviewed by officials, this move will encourage fishers to bend the rules as the risk of being found out is low. It is a move simply to protect the industry that Minister Jones is today reported as saying. The bill reduces and in some cases removes checks and balances on decision-making and enforcement.

Although fishing quota has status as property, our coastal waters and the high seas are a public domain and the environment in these areas is highly valued by all. It is our commons. This bill separates the TAC process from sustainability measures and politicizes this setting process by including social, cultural, and economic factors, allowing for inflating the TAC rather than ensuring it's sustainable.Low information should be a red card to reduce fishing pressure.

Multi-year stock setting seems to be a stab in the dark and hope it all turns out, with no clear criteria or monitoring. There needs to be a more cautious approach to TAC setting, given increasing temperatures of seawater and actual potential effects on climate change on fish stocks. In conclusion, the Fisheries Act does need amendment for better sustainability, better information on which to base harvesting, the banning of bottom trawling, and promotion of marine reserves.
So, the bill is the opposite of what is needed and should be withdrawn. Thank you. Thank you very much for your time and for your submission, Linda. We really appreciate it. Thank you. Thank you.
Emily McDonald.

Oh. Hi. Oh, hi, Emily. Hello. Kia ora. Oh, fantastic. We can see you. Can you see me? Yeah. Kia ora. Yes, cool. Emily McDonald aho. And I've got this. Do you see the painting here? No, not really. Yes, we can see something. Something. That's James K. Baxter, or Christ holding the dying albatross.

It's a painting by Nigel Brown. It's called "Ancient Mariner Lost the Albatross," and it's about us losing our way and the mariner going out to whoever will listen to tell them before it's too late, that we have to save the natural world, and the albatross is signified by the natural world.

Right. And, so I thought this was so important. He says around the painting, "Lost the albatross that made us strong." So, this is in a spiritual sense and in a literal sense, we've got hundreds, possibly millions of albatross who've been killed by fishing, commercial fishing. And I reject the Fishing Amendment Bill in its entirety because I think this will only make their plight so much worse.

This is a species which is just about extinct, and it is just a crisis. The 21st century crisis is the death of the natural world. So, it's up to us now to do something about it before it's too late. And I think Nigel Brown was so good in having a whole exhibition devoted to that.

So, the Ancient Mariner said, "I pass like night from land to land. I have strange power of speech. The moment that his face I see I know the person that must hear me. To them my tale I teach." The tale that the world is dying. And I recommend that the

sea become ngā moana, that it needs personhood because that's possibly the only way that the government will stop exploiting it and seeing it only as a means of making money and swallowing up the ecosystems and leaving dust and debris in its wake.

I support implementing a zero bycatch target, and because Aotearoa is the seabird capital of the world, it's just important that all the sort of long line and bottom trawling is banned. I also think that... What else did I say?

Yeah, that the iwi of Aotearoa believe in the kaupapa of kaitiakitanga, and we believe in manaakitanga and mana motuhake, and yet the government does not believe in the mana of our moana. And I've seen for myself starving seagulls just in Central Auckland, Tāmaki Makaurau.

The Hauraki Gulf, the moana there has been stripped and they've got no kai, no food. They're starving. So that is unacceptable. Totally unacceptable for seagulls, which are our native birds, to be starving. Thank you very much for your very considered contribution.

With the rhyme of the Ancient Mariner, I thought you were talking about Samuel Taylor Coleridge. It was taking me back to sort of fifth form English lessons. That's right. It is Coleridge. Yeah. But fantastic. Thank you very much. Okay. Kia ora koutou katoa. Yeah. Ka kite.Okay, Kiri Mane, is it?
Mane.

Not here? Yep, they have joined. Kiri. Kia ora. Kia ora. Kia ora. Right. You've got five minutes, which goes pretty quickly, so if you want to leave some time for questions, that would be great, but the floor is yours. Okay. Thank you for giving me- Ka pai... the opportunity to share my thoughts.

Tēnā koutou katoa. My name is Kiri Mane. I come before you today not just as a 75-year-old New Zealander, but as a mother, as a nana, and someone who has a deep concern for my mokopuna and the generations who will come after me. I care. I care about our moana. I care about our oceans. I care about our fish.

And I care about whether my mokopuna and their mokopuna will still be able to stand on our beaches, on our whenua, and gather kai the way our tupuna taught us. I absolutely oppose the Fisheries Amendment Bill. I feel it will not only harm our ocean, it will impact on the way we have traditionally survived.

I worry for my children, my mokopuna, and the world they will grow up in. Right now, whānau across Aotearoa are already under pressure. The cost of living is so high, food is expensive, and the economic pressures of today are breaking families apart.

I worry that the decisions that you make will favor business interests over our people's rights when transparency is reduced and protections weakened. The ocean for my whānau is more than just water. It's our pantry. It's our life. It's our whānau. It's a tradition.

It is survival. It is whakapapa. Our children and our mokopuna deserve to inherit healthy, sustainable oceans full of life and not what's left after the five-year timeframe set by the fisheries' decision-makers. These are not minor adjustments.

They are shifts in power. And from where I stand, they risk shifting power away from the public and future generations and closer to those who profit most in the short term. I ask this committee, what will be left for our mokopuna if we continue to walk down this path?

Because this is not only about economics, it is about stewardship. It is about ensuring our fisheries remain abundant, resilient, and accessible. It is about protecting breeding populations so that our children inherit oceans full of life, not empty waters and broken promises.

I'm not here to say commercial fishing has no place. I'm here to say it must never come at the cost of people, our people, our environment, and our future generations. So today, I ask the select committee to reject this bill in its current form, protect public oversight and transparency, strengthen, not weaken, sustainability protections, prioritize abundant fish populations for local food security and future resilience, and urgently

investigate how New Zealand can move towards more selective, environmentally responsible fishing practices. Because this is bigger than policy. This is about our legacy. This is about whether our future generations will inherit a living ocean or simple memories of what we failed to protect.

I stand here today for my mokopuna, for your mokopuna, for all those who cannot stand here yet, but who will live with the consequences of what we choose now. I am a nana who cares, and I ask you to show that you care as well. Ngā mihi nui.
